title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 1)
text:
Annual Update Fine Arts
December 2020
College of the Siskiyous Program Review utilizes a four year cycle comprising three
annual updates and one full review. This Fall 2020 the program will undertake an
Annual Review of the academic year 2019-2020. The principal preparer of the program
review is the Instruction area Dean Valerie Roberts in consultation with the full-time
faculty, Sean Kenny, and Instructional Student Specialist II, Amanda Thomas.
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
Last year (2019-2020) the Fine Arts program at the college produced 49.5 FTES, and
awarded five Associate of Arts degrees. Something that is noteworthy about this data is
that it fits very neatly into our Institutions Guided Pathways Initiative by the fact that
almost all of the FTES produced by the Fine Arts department is for-credit units.
The necessary transition to fully online last spring was difficult for both the student and
the college. This move was due to the global pandemic, COVID-19, emergency
measures. Many were experiencing the rigor of taking classes and/or teaching online for
the first time. System wide, California Community Colleges have experienced a drop in
enrollment due to the pandemic. We have experienced a similar drop in enrollment
(49.5 FTES compared to 57.62 FTES the previous year), which may have been
exacerbated by the reduction in the number of classes we offer.
This, in turn, is affected by our reduction in faculty. For instance, of our Digital Imaging
and Digital Illustration courses taught regularly by Jim Gilmore (now retired) only Digital
Illustration was taught in the 19-20 academic year. These two classes - which provide
graphic design skills directly relevant to employment in our digital age - were

-- 1 of 7 --

title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 2)
text:
consistently offered before then with dependably healthy enrollment. Design and
Sculpture have continued to suffer from scheduling challenges and low enrollment.
In addition to the continued vacancy of two additional full time art faculty, the
Instructional Support Specialist continues to be support by the District at 28 hours/week,
10 months/year. (As noted in previous Program Review and Annual Updates, this
position was 40 hours/week before a series of budget cuts in 2012.) An additional part
time instructor was hired to teach Art History and Photography, reduced the number of
courses no longer offered since a full time faculty retirement, it does add to the variety
of courses our Instructional Support Specialist must support.
While it is hard to make comparisons in our current situation, our courses that are more
easily transitioned online (Art History, Painting, Drawing, etc.) had similar enrollment in
Fall of 2020 when compared to a typical semester of in person instruction. Courses,
such as ceramics, that come with unique safety concerns and rely heavily on physical
equipment have, understandably, not fared as well in this time. Teaching studio art
classes online presents a unique and particular challenge. That our drop in enrollment
has not been larger given the circumstances speaks to our adaptability and resilience
as a program.
Many examples exist that demonstrate how the program is successful. The Fine Art
exhibit spaces around the Weed campus like the McCloud Galleries and the LRC Art
Gallery, in which student art exhibits are the predominant focus, are very popular for
guides showing the college to prospective students and parents, and provide ongoing
interest for our attending student body and staff. This program had been producing 12
to 16 exhibits per semester. Of course, these exhibits came to a halt with the pandemic.
Also halted during this past year has been the Interdisciplinary bridging to music in club
activities and hosting of food events and music activities. These events are one of the
main hubs of student activity on the Weed campus. The emphasis on community and
belonging are integral to its success. Students want access to studios, materials and
each other as avenues to intellectual and personal growth. The COS art program
represents opportunities for immersion into creative processes that students find

-- 2 of 7 --

title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 3)
text:
fascinating and affirming. It represents an area where students’ lives are changed for
the better; lessons learned in the art classes at this college help to build functional,
productive members of society.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
We have little to no direction on how this is supposed to be happening, but are excited
to participate.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
Probably the major obstacle for classes taught in this program is the transition from F2F
to completely online. While instructors were required to take basic online teaching
@ONE courses the transition was not seamless. Communicating, using new technology
for the first time, creating curriculum that works in an online format...etc made for a
difficult experience.
Reinstating our second full time instructor position would provide numerous benefits for
the program. For one, our enrollment is limited by the number of classes we are able to
offer. We have adjunct instructors who are knowledgeable and experienced in Art
History, Photography, Drawing, Design, Digital Imaging, Digital Illustration, Printmaking,
and other disciplines, yet not all of these classes are being offered regularly and
enrollment continues to be an issue for some. Printmaking, a once popular class, has
not been offered in several years due to availability of faculty with this expertise. Our
Art History class has one of the highest enrollments at the college. Additional Art
History classes could be offered in the future but are currently limited due to the course
being taught by a part time instructor. In addition to fulfilling CSU humanities
requirements, like all Fine Arts courses, these courses fulfill Arts and Humanities, Visual
Literacy, and World Cultures GE requirements in the University of California system.
Another benefit to having additional full time faculty is quality of instruction and campus
culture. In studio art classes, the bulk of the work takes place outside of lecture hours.
Coursework in studio art classes takes an immense amount of time, and access to
facilities and equipment is key for many disciplines. Having the instructors or ISS

-- 3 of 7 --

title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 4)
text:
person on hand is essential for student success in studio courses. Having our studio
courses taught by adjunct instructors limits student access and instructors’ ability to fully
and generously engage with students. The simple fact of having an established
instructor present in their office in the art department would provide consistency of
instruction, a relational atmosphere, and the individual attention and instruction that
draws students to small colleges.
In the context of our new building, and our eventual post-COVID return to in-person
instruction, the college has an opportunity to demonstrate its commitment to the arts
and the community by reinstating positions and funding for the art department. College
of the Siskiyous has a responsibility to attend to the creative vitality of our region. Our
new Performing and Fine Arts building has the potential to solidify the College’s position
as a cultural hub for the community. It would be a disservice to our students and the
community at large to inhibit this opportunity with insufficient teaching resources and
program funding.
The Instructional Support Specialist position at only 28 hours/week limits student
support and learning. For example, critiques in the many art courses offered at the
college have performance components that find students creating unique formats in
presentation and conceptual projects. The lack of full time ISS support has had a
negative effect on student success. As mentioned previously, the ISS supports
instruction for all art courses. These courses are varied and require different types of
support (set up, open labs, processing of materials, ordering and inventory, and much
more). Our interest is in providing needed support for student learning. It is also in not
just in maintaining, but in growing the success, health, standards, and stability of the
College of the Siskiyous Fine Arts program. This concept currently sits at odds with the
districts level of support for fine arts at this college.
Other Fine Art program obstacles:
● Awkwardly scheduled classes – because of the number of total hours students
must participate in class, the Fine Arts courses take up a large chunk of time in
students’ schedules. It makes it difficult to find a way to schedule the courses so

-- 4 of 7 --

title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 5)
text:
they do not overlap with other needed courses. In addition, because of the time
periods during which students can, or will, take classes, these high hour courses
must be “fit in to” a schedule that makes it difficult for the ISS to support all studio
classes and for students to take more than one studio class in a semester (this
affects Art majors).
● Aging facilities and outdated equipment in all areas of the program – this includes
everything from a heavily used color printer to worn classrooms. Unfortunately, it
seems that facilities improvements are a low priority for the college.
● Dangerous work environment – there are no first aid kits in studios, we have
outdated and dangerous woodworking equipment, window blinds that do not
functioning and are dangerous to room occupants, and non-institutionalized
maintenance and cleaning for studio classes. Cleaning for the studio classes
has been done by hiring a student worker. Money is requested each year for this
purpose. If funds are not issued, the ISS does much of this work.
● Cuts in essential areas of budget - Between 2015-16 and 2019-20, our student
worker budget has gone from $9,681 to $2,480. Although this issue has been
temporarily avoided this year due to online instruction, this presents a large
problem as we rely on student workers to clean the ceramics studio. Ideally, our
classrooms would be cleaned by the maintenance department, and these funds
would be used to provide art students with more meaningful work experience that
presents a relevant educational opportunity in the arts (i.e. assisting in the photo
lab and gallery, assisting with ceramics studio functions such as loading kilns,
etc.).
Nearly every portion of our budget has been cut significantly over the past 5
years. Instructional supply has gone from $3,000 to $1,900. Maintenance/repair
has gone from $1550 to $1200. Travel, between 2015-16 and 2019-20 went
from $1400 (plus an additional $340 for mileage/school vans) to $300. Our guest
lecturer budget ($400 in 2015-16) and A/V supply budget ($200 in 2015-16) have
also been eliminated. The exception is our environmental safety budget. These
funds were temporarily eliminated and the ISS had to justify the necessity of
these funds for the program. The amount was subsequently increased from

-- 5 of 7 --

title: Fine Arts Annual Program Review COS Fall 2020 (Part 6)
text:
$150 to $450 due to increases in costs for disposal of photo chemicals. The
consistent eliminations and decreases in funds severely limit our options as a
program and drive our focus from a vision of expansion and growth to one of
subsistence.
● Unsure of our budget making it difficult to plan – we often start the academic year
not knowing if requests are funded and our budget amount. Sometimes the
budget varies drastically from the year before, with no explanation.
● Unsure of our institutions priorities making it difficult to plan – it is difficult to
understand why the District funds certain positions and instructional (and non-
instructional) areas but not others and priorities are not clearly communicated to
the campus community.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Submit a CQIP if you are
requesting a budget allocation that falls outside of regular yearly budget allocations).
Reinstating area budget as needed and appropriate (noted above).
Full time ISS. Our immediate need priority is in making our ISS II position full time
again. This position was full time for over 30 years, and a bustling program like the Fine
Arts program needs full time support to help push standards through innovative
approaches to teaching, art making and learning. Students rely heavily on the ISS for
assistance with materials and equipment, access to studio time with support, and
guidance through their creative processes. The cost of making the ISS II position a full
time budgeted position would be approximately $35,000 in salary and benefits.
Considering the FTES production of our program, reinstating a full time art program ISS
position would not significantly impact the area’s bottom line, and would be a huge
benefit to the program and consequent student success.
Additional full time instructor. This would allow more desired and needed courses to be
consistently offered, resulting in more FTES for the college and more opportunity for Art
majors to complete their degree. Cost, depending on starting salary and benefits
package, would be an estimated $105,000.
